title: Jeremiah 36
subtitle: Baruch's Scroll Burned

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Jehoiakim Burns Scroll

sections.header: Verses 1-19 -- The Scroll Written and Read

heb.paragraph: God commands Jeremiah to write all his oracles on a scroll (megilla, v.2). Baruch writes as Jeremiah dictates, then reads the scroll publicly in the temple. The megilla represents the first written collection of prophetic oracles -- a precursor to the biblical book we now possess. The act of writing transforms oral prophecy into permanent Scripture.

refs.note: Ezekiel eats the scroll of lamentation -- the physical consumption of God's written word.
John eats the little scroll -- the same pattern of prophetic identification with the written word.
"All Scripture is God-breathed" -- the doctrine of inspiration that chapters like Jeremiah 36 illustrate in narrative form.

notes.note: The dictation process: Jeremiah speaks, Baruch writes. This is the clearest biblical account of how prophetic books were composed -- oral dictation to a professional scribe.
Jeremiah is "restricted" ('atzur) -- possibly under house arrest or ritually barred from the temple. Baruch becomes the prophet's voice to the public.
Three readings: the people hear and are convicted; the officials hear and are alarmed; the king will hear and be enraged. The scroll provokes different responses at each level of society.

Calvin notes the permanence of writing: "God commands the scroll so that the word cannot be dismissed as misremembered speech. Written text is fixed testimony that outlasts the prophet who spoke it.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Baruch used a "writing" (katobet) instrument on a papyrus or leather scroll. As a professional scribe (sopher), Baruch was trained in standard scribal conventions of the period. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies this as "the most important chapter in the Hebrew Bible for understanding the composition of prophetic books." The dictation, reading, destruction, and rewriting sequence illuminates how Scripture came into being. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads the scroll as "the word becoming text." Oral prophecy is perishable; written prophecy is permanent. The transition from speech to writing is itself a theological act -- God's word is being preserved for future generations. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: The date is the fourth year of Jehoiakim (605-604 BC). God commands a comprehensive scroll of all oracles from 627 BC onward. Baruch reads it three times: in the temple (v.10), to the officials (v.15), and it is then read to the king (v.21). The officials are alarmed and urge Baruch and Jeremiah to hide. The episode preserves a remarkable account of how prophetic literature moved from oral proclamation to written text.

sections.header: Verses 20-32 -- The King Burns the Scroll

heb.paragraph: Jehoiakim cuts the scroll column by column with a scribal knife (ta'ar, v.23) and burns it in the fire pot. The ta'ar was used to cut papyrus and trim reed pens -- a tool of the scribal profession turned against the scribal product. The king uses the instrument of writing to destroy writing. The detail is loaded with irony.

refs.note: Josiah tears his robes when the Book of the Law is read -- the faithful response that Jehoiakim inverts.
"My word will not return to me empty" -- the burned scroll is rewritten, confirming the indestructibility of God's word.

notes.note: The king sits in the winter apartment with a fire pot burning. As each three or four columns are read, he cuts them off and feeds them to the fire. The deliberate pace -- cut, burn, cut, burn -- communicates contempt.
"The king and all his attendants showed no fear nor tore their clothes." The absence of fear is itself the indictment -- compare Josiah's terror in 2 Kings 22:11.
God commands a second scroll. The word survives the fire. What human power tries to suppress, divine power restores with additions.
Judgment on Jehoiakim: "his body will be thrown out" (cf. 22:19, "the burial of a donkey"). The king who burned the scroll will himself be discarded.
Calvin contrasts father and son: "Josiah trembled; Jehoiakim smirked. The same word that melts one heart hardens another. Response to Scripture reveals character."
Calvin celebrates the rewritten scroll: "The fire could not consume what God had spoken. Every attempt to destroy Scripture only multiplies it."

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The "three or four columns" (delathot) are the sections of text on the scroll. Each deleth was a panel or column of writing -- the king cuts each section as it is read, indicating he hears the entire content before burning it. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom reads the scroll-burning as "the most dramatic act of opposition to prophetic authority in the Hebrew Bible." But the rewriting with additions (v.32) demonstrates that persecution amplifies rather than silences the prophetic word. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann identifies a "contest between fire and ink." The king commands fire; God commands rewriting. Fire is temporary; text is permanent. "Many similar words were added" -- persecution produces more Scripture, not less. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: Jehoiakim's response is the antithesis of his father Josiah's. When Josiah heard the Book of the Law, he tore his robes in repentance (2 Kgs 22:11). When Jehoiakim hears the scroll of Jeremiah, he cuts it up and burns it. The contrast is deliberate: father and son respond to God's word in opposite ways. The chapter notes that neither Jehoiakim nor his attendants "showed any fear or tore their clothes" (v.24). But the word cannot be destroyed: Jeremiah dictates the entire scroll again, "and many similar words were added" (v.32).

rows.label: vv.20-26
rows.text: Third Reading: The King Burns the Scroll

lit.note: The scroll episode traces God's word through dictation, reading, burning, and rewriting. The word survives the fire; the king does not survive the word.

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 10 -- the scroll episode is the definitive narrative about the creation and indestructibility of Scripture.

hebtext.note: God commands Jeremiah to write all his oracles on a scroll (megilla, v.2). Baruch writes as Jeremiah dictates, then reads the scroll publicly in the temple. Jehoiakim cuts the scroll column by column with a scribal knife (ta'ar, v.23) and burns it in the fire pot. The ta'ar was used to cut papyrus and trim reed pens -- a tool of the scribal profession turned against the scribal product. The king uses the instrument of writing to destroy writing. trans.title: Comparing translations in Baruch's Scroll Burned

translations.text: So you go to the house of the Lord on a day of fasting and read to the people from the scroll the words of the Lord that you wrote as I dictated. Read them to all the people of Judah who come in from their towns.

so you are to go, and on a day of fasting in the hearing of all the people in the LORD’s house you shall read the words of the LORD from the scroll that you have written at my dictation. You shall read them also in the hearing of all the men of Judah who come out of their cities.
Perhaps they will bring their petition before the Lord and will each turn from their wicked ways, for the anger and wrath pronounced against this people by the Lord are great.”
It may be that their plea for mercy will come before the LORD, and that every one will turn from his evil way, for great is the anger and wrath that the LORD has pronounced against this people.”

thread.text: Ezekiel eats the scroll of lamentation -- the physical consumption of God's written word.
John eats the little scroll -- the same pattern of prophetic identification with the written word.
"All Scripture is God-breathed" -- the doctrine of inspiration that chapters like Jeremiah 36 illustrate in narrative form.
Josiah tears his robes when the Book of the Law is read -- the faithful response that Jehoiakim inverts.
"My word will not return to me empty" -- the burned scroll is rewritten, confirming the indestructibility of God's word.

coaching.tip: God tells Jeremiah to write his prophecies on a scroll — the transition from oral to written prophecy. Baruch writes and reads publicly what Jeremiah speaks. This chapter shows Scripture being made: a prophet dictates, a scribe records, the text circulates. We're watching the mechanics of inspiration. The process is human (dictation, copying, reading aloud) and the authority is divine.

Jehoiakim cuts the scroll with a knife and burns it column by column (v. 23) — calmly, deliberately, in front of the fire. No one trembles except three officials who urge him to stop. Then God tells Jeremiah: 'Take another scroll and write on it all the words that were on the first scroll' (v. 28). You can burn the scroll; you cannot burn the word. The text survives the king who destroyed it. The king does not survive the text.

prayer_prompt: As King Jehoiakim cuts and burns the scroll of God's word column by column, consider asking God whether you have ever dismissed parts of His message that made you uncomfortable rather than letting them change you.
